skill.name: neoantigen-predictor
skill.description: Predict patient-specific neoantigen candidate peptides from HLA typing and tumor mutation profiles for cancer immunotherapy research.
skill.summary: Predicts neoantigen candidates using MHC binding affinity and immunogenicity scoring from HLA and mutation data.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 128 potential issues, but review confirms nearly all are false positives caused by the scanner misclassifying bioinformatics algorithm references as cryptographic operations and markdown code blocks as shell execution. The skill is a legitimate, well-documented research tool for neoantigen prediction. The only confirmed findings are hardcoded URLs to legitimate scientific databases in documentation files (low risk) and a standard file write operation in main.py for output results.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded External URLs in Documentation Files
low_findings.description: Documentation files contain hardcoded URLs pointing to external scientific databases (Ensembl, UniProt, IEDB, TCGA) and bioinformatics tools (NetMHCpan, MHCflurry). These are legitimate reference links in a research skill and do not represent data exfiltration or malicious endpoints. URLs are in documentation only, not in executable code.

low_findings.title: File System Write Operation for Output Results
low_findings.description: main.py writes prediction results to a user-specified output file path. This is expected and documented behavior for a data analysis tool that produces result files. The file path is provided via command-line argument, not hardcoded. No unauthorized or hidden file access detected.

content.user_title: Predict Neoantigens for Cancer Immunotherapy Research
content.value_statement: Researchers struggle to identify which tumor mutations produce peptides that the immune system can recognize, a key step in personalized cancer immunotherapy. This skill predicts neoantigen candidates from patient HLA typing and mutation profiles using MHC binding affinity prediction and immunogenicity scoring to prioritize targets for validation.
actual_capabilities: Predicts MHC class I binding affinity for patient-specific HLA alleles using anchor residue scoring and physicochemical property analysis
Generates variant peptides from protein mutation data using VCF, CSV table, or FASTA peptide sequence input formats
Calculates immunogenicity scores based on peptide foreignness, anchor position mutations, and hydrophobicity changes
Ranks neoantigen candidates by a priority score that combines MHC binding affinity, immunogenicity, and clinical relevance
Filters results by binding rank threshold or binding level (Strong, Weak, Non-binder) for downstream analysis
Outputs structured results in JSON or CSV format with binding predictions and summary statistics
limitations: MHC binding prediction uses a simplified simulation model rather than the full NetMHCpan neural network for production-grade accuracy
Immunogenicity prediction has approximately 60-70% correlation with experimental results and requires in vitro or in vivo validation
Does not consider HLA molecule expression levels on the cell surface or tumor microenvironment factors
Cannot predict immune tolerance, regulatory T cell responses, or clinical treatment outcomes

use_cases.title: Identify Neoantigen Candidates for Vaccine Design
use_cases.description: A cancer researcher inputs patient HLA typing and tumor sequencing mutation data to generate a ranked list of neoantigen candidates for personalized vaccine development.

use_cases.title: Screen Mutation Peptides for T Cell Therapy Targets
use_cases.description: A bioinformatician screens a panel of tumor-specific mutations against common HLA alleles to identify high-affinity peptide targets for TCR-T cell therapy development.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Mutations for Experimental Validation
use_cases.description: A translational scientist uses the priority scoring system to select the top neoantigen candidates from a large mutation set for costly ELISPOT or tetramer validation assays.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Neoantigen Prediction
prompt_templates.prompt: Run a basic neoantigen prediction using the neoantigen-predictor skill. Use HLA alleles HLA-A*02:01 and HLA-A*11:01 with the mutation data from the attached CSV file. Output the top candidates with binding rank below 2%.
prompt_templates.scenario: First-time user wanting a quick prediction from standard inputs

prompt_templates.title: Custom HLA and Mutation Input
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a patient with HLA typing A*02:01, B*07:02, C*04:01 and the following mutations from tumor sequencing: TP53 R273H and KRAS G12D. Predict neoantigen candidates with peptide lengths 9 and 10 using the netmhcpan method.
prompt_templates.scenario: Researcher with specific patient data and known mutations

prompt_templates.title: Filtered Results with Threshold Tuning
prompt_templates.prompt: Run neoantigen prediction with the attached VCF file and HLA list. Use a rank cutoff of 0.5 for strong binders only. Generate output in CSV format and list only the top 10 candidates ranked by priority score.
prompt_templates.scenario: Scientist who needs high-confidence candidates for limited validation resources

prompt_templates.title: Comprehensive Immunogenicity Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the neoantigen-predictor skill to analyze these mutations with full immunogenicity assessment. Report the foreignness scores, anchor mutation status, and hydrophobicity changes for each candidate. Explain which mutations are most likely to generate an immune response based on the scoring algorithm.
prompt_templates.scenario: Immunologist evaluating multiple mutation candidates for immune response potential

output_examples.input: Patient with HLA-A*02:01 and TP53 R273H mutation (standard VCF input)
output_examples.output: The prediction identified 12 strong-binding peptide candidates from the TP53 R273H mutation across HLA-A*02:01. The top candidate was a 9mer peptide spanning the mutation site with a binding rank of 0.12% and estimated affinity of 34.5 nM. The immunogenicity score was 0.87 due to the arginine-to-histidine change at an anchor residue position, making this a high-priority candidate for experimental validation.

output_examples.input: Three mutations (TP53 R273H, KRAS G12D, EGFR L858R) against HLA-A*02:01, HLA-A*11:01, HLA-B*07:02
output_examples.output: The analysis evaluated 156 peptide-HLA combinations across the three mutations and three HLA alleles. Of these, 12 were classified as strong binders (rank below 0.5%), 44 as weak binders, and 100 as non-binders. The KRAS G12D mutation produced the highest proportion of strong-binding candidates for HLA-A*11:01. The EGFR L858R mutation showed lower overall binding affinity but higher foreignness scores, suggesting distinct immune recognition potential.

best_practices: Validate top-ranked neoantigen candidates experimentally using ELISPOT or tetramer assays before clinical use
Provide both wild-type and variant peptide sequences to enable accurate foreignness scoring and self-tolerance assessment
Combine prediction results with tumor expression data and variant allele frequency to prioritize candidates most likely to be clinically relevant
anti_patterns: Using prediction results as sole evidence for clinical treatment decisions without experimental validation or regulatory approval
Running predictions with incomplete or inaccurate HLA typing data, which produces unreliable binding affinity results
Ignoring the simulation notice and assuming predicted binding affinities match real NetMHCpan output without verification

faq.question: What input data do I need to run neoantigen prediction?
faq.answer: You need patient HLA typing data (such as HLA-A*02:01) and tumor mutation data. Mutations can be provided as a VCF file, a CSV table with gene and protein change columns, or a FASTA file with variant peptide sequences.

faq.question: How accurate is the MHC binding prediction?
faq.answer: The built-in prediction uses a simplified simulation model based on anchor residue preferences and physicochemical properties. It is not as accurate as NetMHCpan 4.1, which requires local installation. The simulation is best for screening and educational purposes.

faq.question: Can I use this for clinical decision-making?
faq.answer: No. This tool is for research purposes only. All predictions require experimental validation, and results should not be the sole basis for clinical decisions about patient treatment.

faq.question: What HLA alleles are supported?
faq.answer: The predictor supports common MHC class I molecules including HLA-A (01:01, 02:01, 03:01, 11:01, 24:02, and others), HLA-B (07:02, 08:01, 27:05, 35:01, 57:01, and others), and HLA-C alleles. Mouse MHC molecules H2-Db, H2-Kb, H2-Kd, and H2-Ld are also supported.

faq.question: What output formats are available?
faq.answer: Results can be generated in JSON format with full prediction details or CSV format for spreadsheet analysis. The JSON output includes binding affinity, immunogenicity scores, and priority rankings for each candidate.

faq.question: Does this skill make external network calls?
faq.answer: The Python script itself does not make external network calls. The requests library is listed as a dependency but is not used in the current implementation. Reference URLs in the documentation point to scientific databases for the user to consult separately.
